question_text: The Wohl degradation, shown below, can be used to convert an aldose into another aldose with one fewer carbon. Give the structure of the missing compounds as well as the curved-arrow mechanisms for the conversion of B to C and C to arabinose. Reaction sequence involves: starting aldose reacted with NH2OH to form A (an oxime), then excess Ac2O converts A into B (C18H25O12N), then NaOAc converts B to C (compound with nitrile group C≡N), then NaOCH3 in CH3OH converts C to arabinose. Show the mechanisms for conversion of B to C focusing on formation of the nitrile and C to arabinose focusing on how the aldehyde is formed.
canonical_answer: Structures of the missing compounds are shown in the provided scheme: A (oxime form), B (acetylated oxime), and C (nitrile compound). The mechanism for B to C involves base-promoted elimination of the oxime hydroxyl to form a nitrile via intramolecular attacks as shown (curved arrows for migration of electrons from N=OAc to form N≡C). The conversion of C to arabinose involves nucleophilic attack of methoxide, formation of an intermediate, and hydrolysis leading to formation of aldehyde group in arabinose.

question_text: Consider the structure of raffinose, a trisaccharide found in sugar beets, beans, cabbage, and other plants. Classify raffinose as a reducing or nonreducing sugar, and use abbreviated structures to support your claim. Reaction: raffinose with Br2 at pH 5-6 in H2O shows no reaction due to absence of hemiacetal carbons, indicating acetal carbons (not oxidizable). Bonus: Name one of the constituent monosaccharides found within raffinose.
canonical_answer: Raffinose is a non-reducing sugar; Bonus: D-glucose; D-galactose; or D-fructose

error_comment: Task requires drawing and is not text-oriented

question_text:
A) Classify the following aldose as D or L. The aldose is shown with a Fischer projection having CHO, H, OH and CH3 substituents arranged vertically, with a note to choose/select placeholder {box_1}.

B) Which pair of the following aldoses (A, B, C as Fischer projections) are epimers and which pair are enantiomers? Compounds A, B, and C are shown with substituents (OH, H, CH2OH, CHO) in different configurations. The question asks to assign epimers and enantiomers.

Bonus: Which pair of compounds are diastereomers? Choose/select between B and C or A and C.
canonical_answer: A) L; B) Compounds A and C are epimers. Compounds A and B are enantiomers.; Bonus: B and C

question_text: (+)-Trehalose, C12H22O11, is a non-reducing sugar found in young mushrooms and is the energy source for certain flying insects. It gives only D-glucose when hydrolyzed by aqueous acid or by α-glucosidase. It is inert to β-glucosidase. Methylation with excess methyl iodide gives an octa-O-methyl derivative that, upon hydrolysis, yields only 2,3,4,6-tetra-O-methyl-D-glucose. Explain the experimental data with annotated chemical structures. What is the structure of (+)-trehalose? Represent your answer using a Haworth representation. Bonus: Provide a common name for the polysaccharide shown on the cover of the quiz.
canonical_answer: Structure is an α,α-1,1-linked disaccharide of two D-glucose units (shown in the boxed Haworth structure). Bonus answer: starch

question_text: An aldopentose, O, is oxidized to a diacid, P, which is optically active. Compound O is also Ruff-degraded to an aldotetrose, Q, which undergoes oxidation to an optically inactive diacid, R. Assuming that O has the D-configuration, what are the structures of O, P, Q, and R? Represent your answers in each box by using Fischer projections displayed in the standard carbohydrate orientation. Bonus: List the reagent(s) necessary to effect Ruff degradation of compound O to compound Q. Reagent options given are: 1. Br2, H2O (can also include Ca(OH)2 or CaCO3) and 2. H2O2, Fe2SO4 or H2O2, Fe(OAc)3.
canonical_answer: O= D-aldopentose (Fischer shown); P= corresponding diacid of O; Q= D-aldotetrose (Ruff degraded product of O); R= diacid from oxidation of Q; Bonus reagents: Br2, H2O (with Ca(OH)2 or CaCO3), H2O2, Fe2SO4 or H2O2, Fe(OAc)3
